title: Magna Carta
summary: The king's power is formally limited.
what: King John accepted a charter limiting royal authority and recognising that the monarch was not above the law.
why: It became a founding myth of English liberties, constitutional restraint and the rule of law.
colle: Can a symbolic historical text still shape modern democracy?

title: The Anglican Reformation
summary: Henry VIII breaks with Rome.
what: The Act of Supremacy made the monarch head of the Church of England.
why: The King remains Supreme Governor of the established Church of England.
colle: Is an established church compatible with a modern, secular society?

title: Glorious Revolution & Bill of Rights
summary: Parliamentary supremacy is consolidated.
what: James II was removed and William and Mary accepted limits on royal power.
why: The event established the basis of constitutional monarchy and parliamentary sovereignty.
colle: Does Britain need a written constitution?

title: Act of Union
summary: England and Scotland form Great Britain.
what: The English and Scottish Parliaments were united at Westminster.
why: The survival of this Union is now questioned by Scottish nationalism and Brexit.
colle: Is the United Kingdom still genuinely united?

title: Union with Ireland
summary: The United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland is created.
what: Ireland was formally incorporated into the United Kingdom.
why: The later partition of Ireland and Northern Ireland's status remain central political questions.
colle: Could Irish reunification reshape the UK?

title: Abolition of the slave trade
summary: Britain bans the transatlantic slave trade.
what: British ships were prohibited from transporting enslaved people, although slavery itself continued in the Empire.
why: The date is central to debates over national pride, responsibility and the memory of slavery.
colle: How should Britain remember both its role in slavery and in abolition?

title: Abolition of slavery in the Empire
summary: Slavery is abolished across most of the British Empire.
what: The Slavery Abolition Act emancipated enslaved people in most British colonies.
why: Compensation went mainly to slave owners, a fact now used in debates about reparations.
colle: Should former colonial powers pay reparations?

title: The Victorian era
summary: Industrial power and imperial expansion reach their height.
what: Britain became the “workshop of the world” and ruled a vast empire under Queen Victoria.
why: The era shaped Britain's global influence, class structure, cities and imperial memory.
colle: Is imperial nostalgia preventing Britain from facing its past?

title: The First World War
summary: A generation is marked by mass warfare and loss.
what: Britain fought with the Allies in a conflict that caused enormous military and civilian sacrifice.
why: Remembrance culture and the poppy remain major elements of British public life.
colle: How do national rituals shape collective memory?

title: The Second World War
summary: Churchill, the Blitz and Britain's “finest hour”.
what: Britain resisted Nazi Germany, endured the Blitz and emerged victorious but exhausted and indebted.
why: WWII remains one of the strongest sources of British national mythology.
colle: Can heroic national myths distort present-day political choices?

title: The Beveridge Report
summary: A blueprint for the post-war welfare state.
what: William Beveridge proposed tackling want, disease, ignorance, squalor and idleness.
why: The report laid the intellectual foundations of the modern British welfare state.
colle: How much responsibility should the state assume for citizens' welfare?

title: Creation of the NHS
summary: Universal healthcare becomes a national institution.
what: The National Health Service began providing healthcare free at the point of use and funded through taxation.
why: The NHS is both a symbol of social solidarity and a service under severe pressure.
colle: Can the NHS be reformed without betraying its founding promise?

title: The Empire Windrush arrives
summary: A landmark in post-war immigration.
what: Caribbean migrants arrived to help rebuild Britain after the war.
why: Windrush symbolises multicultural Britain and the continuing legacy of Empire.
colle: Is immigration a challenge to British identity, or one of its foundations?

title: The Suez Crisis
summary: A symbol of Britain's declining global power.
what: Britain, France and Israel intervened in Egypt but withdrew under international pressure.
why: Suez exposed Britain's dependence on the United States and the end of superpower status.
colle: What remains of British global power today?

title: The UK joins the EEC
summary: Britain enters the European project.
what: The UK joined the European Economic Community after years of debate and failed applications.
why: Membership began a difficult relationship with Europe that culminated in Brexit.
colle: Why has Britain's relationship with Europe always been so uneasy?

title: Margaret Thatcher becomes Prime Minister
summary: The neoliberal turn begins.
what: Thatcher launched privatisation, deregulation, tax cuts and confrontation with trade unions.
why: Thatcherism permanently reshaped the economy and Britain's political vocabulary.
colle: Did Thatcher modernise Britain or deepen its inequalities?

title: The miners' strike
summary: A decisive defeat for organised labour.
what: Coal miners fought pit closures in a bitter year-long confrontation with Thatcher's government.
why: The defeat accelerated deindustrialisation and weakened trade union power.
colle: How does economic change reshape communities and political loyalties?

title: New Labour comes to power
summary: Tony Blair promises a “Third Way”.
what: Labour returned after eighteen years, combining market economics with social investment.
why: New Labour introduced devolution and reshaped the centre-left, but Iraq damaged its legacy.
colle: Can centre-left parties combine market efficiency with social justice?

title: The Good Friday Agreement
summary: A peace settlement for Northern Ireland.
what: The agreement created power-sharing institutions and largely ended the Troubles.
why: Brexit complicated the border question and tested the settlement's stability.
colle: Can peace survive when constitutional identities remain unresolved?

title: Devolution takes effect
summary: New institutions open in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland.
what: Holyrood, the Welsh Assembly and Stormont received powers from Westminster.
why: Devolution granted autonomy but also strengthened national identities and independence movements.
colle: Has devolution saved the Union or weakened it?

title: The Supreme Court is created
summary: The highest court becomes institutionally separate from Parliament.
what: The new Supreme Court replaced the judicial role of the Law Lords.
why: It strengthened the visible separation of powers and later played a major role during Brexit.
colle: Should judges be able to restrain elected governments?

title: Scottish independence referendum
summary: Scotland votes 55% to remain in the UK.
what: Scottish voters rejected independence, but the issue remained politically alive.
why: Scotland's later Remain vote in the Brexit referendum revived demands for another vote.
colle: Should Scotland be allowed to hold another referendum?

title: The Brexit referendum
summary: 51.9% vote to leave the European Union.
what: Leave won after a campaign centred on sovereignty, immigration and “taking back control”.
why: The referendum exposed deep territorial, social and generational divisions.
colle: Was Brexit primarily a vote about Europe or about British identity?

title: The UK leaves the European Union
summary: Brexit becomes legally effective.
what: The UK formally left the EU on 31 January 2020, with the transition ending later that year.
why: The consequences for trade, immigration, Northern Ireland and Britain's world role remain major debates.
colle: Has Brexit delivered the control and opportunities promised?

title: Elizabeth II dies; Charles III succeeds
summary: A historic royal transition.
what: Queen Elizabeth II died after a seventy-year reign and Charles III became King.
why: The transition reopened debates about monarchy, continuity, cost and relevance.
colle: Can the monarchy remain relevant to younger generations?

title: Labour returns to power
summary: Keir Starmer becomes Prime Minister.
what: Labour won the general election after fourteen years of Conservative-led government.
why: The change marked a major political reset amid pressure on public services, living standards and trust.
colle: Can a change of government restore trust in British politics?
